sentences.text: In the last time of the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the Russian broke through the front of the Germans in Uman, the German army undertook a great retreat operation into Moldova, into Romania.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [INAUDIBLE] Podolsk, as Kamenetz-Podolsk, which is another city on the Dniester, became the key positions for this retreat, all the German and Romanian army, which were in Ukraine still or in Transnistria, had to withdraw on one of these two points.
So the Germans took over the entire operation they were commanding.
And to tell you the truth, I never saw an army retreating in such an order like the German army.
Was unbelievable.
Units, equipments, guns, everything in perfect order.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I was working at that time in repairing the highways, the roofs, so the-- the access should be better, also is that [INAUDIBLE] organization.
But the group that we worked in on the bridges, they had also some SS people as possible.
These were olderly-- elderly persons, mobilized during the war in Germany, and there was only they worked.
And at the given day, they called me and some other people in and told us-- told us, sneak home.
The SS is coming.
At hike as fast as you can.
And so we did.
We didn't walk on the-- on the roads-- on the routes, but through the fields and so on.
And we somehow got back into the ghetto.

But then, the ghetto was over-flooded with German soldiers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were terribly afraid because we had some knowledge what happened with other places at the retreat of the Germans.
There were also Italian units, and we also looked for to be with the Italians.
They were less bad than the Germans.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But in the last few days, everybody of us wanted to have a German officer in our house, because it was a kind of a protection for drunkards or of all kind of hooligans and so on which were in the German army.
And it was amazing to see, under these conditions on the ghetto, the-- the German soldiers coming with a book to read, toothpaste and the toothbrush like-- and the pajamas and all.
It was amazing.
But things became very hot.
And about a day earlier-- about a day early, the event I'm going to tell you, the SS came-- came in.
We know now that they had the order to exterminate the ghetto in 24 or 48 hours.

But [INAUDIBLE] Shabbat-- it was in March, 1944, but I remember it was [INAUDIBLE] Shabbat-- we heard from the loudspeakers from the bridge because the Germans-- they had there, by the way, quarrel with the Romanian.
Who says which unit will go first?
Because they all run for their life.
So that very loud-- strong, loud speakers, we heard that there is a lot of agitation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father and myself, maybe we're-- I'm not sure we were the only one but very-- from the very few-- we went on the roof of the casino up, and we watched what's going on.
There was a lot of light because there were those big projectors to the bridge.
And suddenly we heard in German language, [GERMAN].
Ivan, that was the-- the German called the Russians Ivan, and the Russians called the German Fritz.
Ivan is on the mountain.
There was a mountain the side of-- that side.
The bridges will be exploding in 10 minutes.
In 10 minutes, the bridges explode, both bridges, with hundreds, perhaps thousands of people on the bridges.
With horses and wagons and tanks and ever.
Everything went in the air, because they didn't want the Russian to get the bridges.
Some of the people jumped into the water, some didn't know to swim.
Some drowned.
Some-- it was terrible.
I saw-- I saw the-- the explosions.
I couldn't see what that-- we heard the-- the noises.
People crying, people yelling, everything.
And suddenly, there was silence, and all the German soldiers who were still in houses and streets and [INAUDIBLE], they start to the Dniester to find some way to get over the Dniester.
Naturally, that only few of them did-- could do it.
But meanwhile, youngsters of the ghetto took possession of the German armies.
And some of them just shot a lot of soldiers.
Some others took prisoners.
We handed over to the Russian army between 20 and 30,000 prisoners from that area that we caught with the German armies.
Later, a monument was erected in Mogilev in memory of 11 partisans who sneaked in with the German army, in German uniforms, on those bridges, and started to throw hand grenades on the bridges.
And therefore, the Germans thought that the Russians are here.
And therefore, they exploded the bridges.
And so, the ghetto was saved.
Those who remained in that ghetto were saved and continued to live.
That's a-- a miracle in [NON-ENGLISH] that none of us can explain.
If that would have taken two, three days more, I don't think that we could take this video today.
What was also amazing, we saw such an armies withdrawing.
They will come.
The Russian army.
That must be something outstanding.
Sunday afternoon around 4:00, a little tank on the mountain, gave three, four shots, [INAUDIBLE].
Three, four shots, and went down.
And now there are about three tanks.
These chased the Russ-- the German army.
And then started to-- to come from the mountains soldiers by foot, by horse, by cars, by you name it, in a total mess.
But masses of soldiers.
Their kitchens and their-- their-- they came two, three weeks later.
They used to go to the-- to a-- to a ground, take a big onion out and-- and clean it from the-- from the mud, and eat it.
They didn't have anything.
We didn't understand how could they chase such a well-organized army, but that's a fact.
I remember razzias in two periods.
Basically in two periods.
One is at the beginning where they just used to catch in people in the street and to chase them farther.
One of my uncles, my mother-- my father's sister-- her name is Raisel.
She's now over 90 years, still living in Jerusalem.
Her husband's name was Israel Heller, H-E-L-L-E-R.
He was from Suceava, and he has been deported from Mogilev-Podol'skii.
He Was caught on the street and deported to what was called the camp of death, in a place-- in a place called Picioara, P-I-C-I-O-A-R-A.
From there, almost nobody came out alive.
Somehow, he succeeded to run away from there, and he arrived, after two weeks of wandering from there, back to the ghetto of Mogilev, and after a day, he passed away from starvation.
That his memory be blessed.
That is one period.
Then there were-- they used to catch people in the street and see if he has documents.
A document means-- meant also that you're wear you work, therefore we are-- therefore I was such a big perfume producer, the second after Christian Dior.

[LAUGHS] And-- and at the end, in the last-- after the different breakthrough from Uman and from-- from [INAUDIBLE], they used to-- to make razzias very frequently and we used to hide in sewage canals.
Everybody had some hiding place, and it was always the problem how to provide food because when you heard something, you had to run.
You couldn't have things to carry with you or you didn't even have things what to carry.
That was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] terrible, and sometimes it had repercussions also on those people who were working.
I remember one night-- we used to work a-- two groups.
A day group for 12 hours, and another group for 12 hours.
And it was terrible.
You had-- first I was shuffling gravel off such a big rubber-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how do you call it?
Which turned a rubber line.
Conveyor belt?
Yes, and-- and this line used to throw the gravel 200, 300 meters where they needed it.
So [INAUDIBLE] we were-- you [INAUDIBLE] and you put gravel.
After half an hour, an hour, you couldn't do it anymore.
So if you straightened out, you got hit by the German soldiers who were around you.
These constructions were all done by German soldiers.
And one time, I had to remain for the second-- that means also for the night-- because some people didn't come-- show up, and they didn't catch enough people because everybody was hiding.
So they had-- they need 1,000 workers.
They got 800, so 200 continued to work, and I was one of those.
And that's another story, but somehow I survived this also.
It was not easy, as you can well imagine.
Dis-- disappearing?
Oh, for nothing.
For any-- not for nothing.
For anything.
Because you looked, or you didn't salute.
You-- you looked at the soldier, then you offended the Romanian army.
So that's good.
And then just because we were-- you were-- he met you in the street for some reason and he didn't like you.
There was no special reason.
You didn't have to do anything.
You could get it for free without any-- any problem.
That was now-- and naturally when they caught one in the raids or in the razzia to be chased somewhere to be deported from the ghetto, the entire way they were hitting for different reasons.
Because there were too slow, because too-- they-- they saw that maybe they-- they want to-- to sneak apart or whatever.
That's-- I can fill up a book just with reasons what-- why to get hit.
They were reported when they felt that the ghetto was overloaded, overcrowded.
No, no, no.
There was no TV announcement or whatever like this.
They were told that they have to go.
And they ended up sometimes in the places where the Romanian army wanted them, and sometimes they really succeeded to sneak away.
And some of them came back into the ghetto, and some of them remained in some villages where they had food more available because they were rural part of the country there.
You could not predict what would happen with you today or tomorrow, and you just lived today.
And what my father did in this occasion to many other-- in this occasion that I told you and many other occasions-- that he never lost his hope for light at tomorrow.
Even when the Russians-- the German were at Stalingrad, and Western Europe was occupied, and the Scandinavian, Norwegian, [INAUDIBLE] was occupied, and the Balkans were occupied.
You couldn't see a spark of light anywhere.
He was full of light in his heart.
And his hope-- and he used to say to me, [NON-ENGLISH] When a Jew wakes up in the morning he has to know that today will be better than yesterday, and tomorrow will be even better.
And with this slogan, we went through all this terrible period and I keep it to my heart until nowadays, and I always am optimistic that tomorrow will be a better day.
Not organized, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occasionally.
I helped many sick people in different-- and whenever I had occasion, I used to be helpful, but not in an organized way.
But somehow I had an association for negativity, a little thing that's interesting, I think, for such a video.
We had-- two little things.
One thing, we didn't have a calendar.
My late father, [INAUDIBLE], was a master in calendar, and we wrote every year, by hand, 10 calendars, which were given out, I don't know to whom.
If I would find one of these calendars, I would pay a fortune.
That's one thing.
And the second problem, we had Pesach.
There were no matzot.
So, wine, you could buy raisins and make some wine.
But how do you make matzot?
So we used to buy little boxes called [INAUDIBLE] boxes-- wheat.
At each one of them to-- to select if it wasn't broken, which means it was [NON-ENGLISH], and to put aside those who were still complete.
And with coffee mills, we made-- we made some flour and we baked matzos in the ghetto.
And we had some matzos for-- for-- for the first night and so on.
That is unforgettable, the effort that has been done just to perform the mitzvah of [NON-ENGLISH], matzo.
It was not [NON-ENGLISH], but it was kosher matzo.
Any what?
Except we I had to work.
In the house, we observed Shabbos.
But when I had to work, I had to work.
We had all the Yom Tovim in the ghetto.
I remember all of them.
Once again, it was centered around our little Sefer Torah.
Hoshanas were no problems.
A lulav, I don't remember having one.
I remember, one of the years there was-- the Vizhnitz Rebbe sent a lulav into [INAUDIBLE], and we walked by foot kilometers to the house of a man who was called [?
Schlomovitz. ?] He was a Vizhnitz [INAUDIBLE] and a representative of the [INAUDIBLE].
And the other-- he had a lulav in [INAUDIBLE], and we could make the [INAUDIBLE] on the lulav [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
In Mogilev.
No, it was in the ghetto.
It was in the ghetto.
It wasn't outside of the ghetto.
The ghetto was a-- a big compound.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this I remember.
But not all the years we had this.
Shavuot is no problem.
I don't remember if we sat in a sukkah or not.
I don't remember that.
I don't remember sitting in a sukkah, but maybe.
From October, 1941 to the liberation of the Russian.
It was March, 1944.
In June or July, 1944 we rented a train wagon-- two train wagons-- a number of families together, and we went to Czernowitz.
No, under the rations we make-- could already make-- I told you-- I didn't.
I telled you, but-- I was working, for instance, in cutting tabak.
We used-- there was a man who had some funds.
He used to buy leaves of tabak, and used to pay us some pennies to cut the tabak.
And with that we made a living.
My father was working.
I was working.
Maybe some of my little sisters worked something.
We made, somehow-- I mean, we didn't go to [INAUDIBLE] Avenue for shopping, but we made, somehow, a living.
And we had some money that we could pay for renting the wagons.
That is a story.
I don't know if you have time for that stories.
But we went, and we had to go to Czernowitz.
At the given point, these two wagons had to be detached from the train and connected to another train which went to Czernowitz.
But they just forgot to do it, so we went-- we were attached to a train who went to the front, to Iasi-- next to Iasi.
And we came to a place which is called, in Bessarabia, Belz.

And that was a big station, so my father and another Jew from the other-- from the other wagon said, let's go here to the commander of the train station and we shall try to get us back, that we have to look on the front.
And in Belz, we heard already the cannons from the front shooting.
And he went down, and as he went into the office, the train moved, and they remained there.
And we moved away.
We didn't know what happened to them.
We didn't know how to get together.
They-- you don't have anything there.
Didn't have any documents, no nothing.
And at the given station, they probably arranged it by phone that these two wagons were detached at a given station.
And the-- the chief of the station, the commander of the station, told them, take this train.
It goes in the same direction.
But this train didn't stop at that station, so they went to another station.
And so they ended up in the front.
And they were caught-- these two Jews with beards-- on the front.
They were brought to a commander, to [INAUDIBLE], a Russian commander, and he was Jewish.
He knew he-- he knew immediately they are not the great spies, these two guys.
[LAUGHTER]
So he gave them an escort, and they were brought back to the station where the wagons were, and when they-- they-- we were preparing to move back, we saw them coming.
So we yelled to them, come, come.
They started to run.
We managed to bring them up into the wagons, and we went back to Czernowitz.
Where did you go from there?
To Czer-- in Czernowitz.
So in Czernowitz, we tried to find our way back to Vatra Dornei, to our home.
But the Russians, they didn't let us do this.
At the given moment, we got word from the [NON-ENGLISH] that we are allowed to introduce a petition to Stalin.
But this petition couldn't be signed only by members of the same family, because you are not allowed to organize yourselves.
So every family fills out a form, and we send it to Stalin.
And it took a few months.
Finally, we got the authorization, all of us, that our Romanian citizenship to get back to Romania.
Romania became good friends with Russia at that time after-- so in-- in-- in-- 23rd of August, 1944, and so we came to the borderline by rail with horses or with oxes.
I don't remember.
And I told you this story, this little story, with the Sefer Torah and the [INAUDIBLE] and the books.
And we went over into Romania to a place called Siret, or Seret, in Yiddish, S-I-R-ET.
And there I found already a Jewish, Zionist organization.
There was-- at that time, it was real democracy in Romania, not this it became later on.
And after a few days we-- we hired a wagon with horses.
It took us three days and three nights to go from Siret to Vatra Dornei, and so we came home-- back.
It was terrible.
First of all, it was a city, and we knew each other.
It was-- it's a little town of 2,000 people.
You know everybody there.
So many didn't come back.
Empty houses.
Houses in which people-- some people didn't come back.
Our house was-- we couldn't go back to our house.
We went just to look.
Maybe we'll find something because before being deported, we buried in the ground some gold coins that we had, all kind of thing that my parents had.
So we went there.
This [?
Stefan ?] emptied out everything.
We didn't find anything.
So we rented some house in another place.
It was called in a Yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH], because there was a family, Yeshiva, which were wealthy persons, and they had their houses.
It was called Yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH].
And there we stayed until-- my parents stayed there until they-- they [INAUDIBLE].
What was it like dealing with the non-Jews of the area once you came back?
I didn't deal anything with them.
[INAUDIBLE] after them.
It was calm.
They knew already that the Jews coming back.
They knew that they are under Russian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] administration.
The-- it was a democratic government, to some extent, at least.
We weren't-- they didn't do anything, but they were no-- at least from my point of view, I don't remember of any special relations with non-Jewish at that time.
In-- that was in the end of 19-- 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was appointed as a member of the National [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Di-- Direction of the [INAUDIBLE] Youth Movement.
By the way, when I came back home, immediately we founded the [INAUDIBLE] Youth Movement.
We organized Zionist youth movement.
It was a very nice sniff, as we called it, branch and we had a lot of activities there.
So they probably, because they heard that there is such a guy in Dornavatra, Vatra Dornei.
So they invited me-- they elected me as a member of the-- and I went to Bucharest.
And there starts a-- another period of my life, which was one of the nicest periods of my life, and this is in the youth movement, [INAUDIBLE] Direction.
And then I became, also, a member of the Haganah later, and I worked in organizing Chalutzim to go to Israel, and organizing illegal transports to Israel, what's called [NON-ENGLISH].
And how I went to Israel, that's another story.
